title: The ball that cheats
intro: A thinner gate oxide grips the channel harder — a sharper, better-behaved hill. So thin it to the limit, right? Watch the wall as you do: below ~0.8 nm of equivalent oxide, balls start APPEARING ON THE OTHER SIDE without ever going over. That is not a bug. Find the oxide thickness that keeps both leaks — over the hill and through the wall — quiet.
explain: Mechanics just ended. A classical ball never crosses a barrier taller than its energy; an electron is a wave with a tail that leaks THROUGH thin barriers — quantum tunneling. Gate direct tunneling grows ~10× for every 0.25 nm of SiO₂-equivalent thinning: a hard exponential wall that no clever electrostatics fixes. This is why high-k metal gate happened at the 45 nm node (2007): a high-k dielectric keeps the PHYSICAL barrier thick while acting electrically thin. In this simplified model you can only trade one leak against the other — real engineers changed the material.

choices: Balls occasionally appear through the wall

prediction.prompt: You thin the oxide wall far below 1 nm. What do you expect to see the balls do?
